title: Conjunctivitis
oneLiner: Acute or chronic inflammation of the conjunctiva, most often infectious or allergic; common, usually self-limiting, but mimics sight-threatening emergencies.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 22-year-old medical student presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of red, gritty left eye. She reports waking with eyelids stuck together this morning. There is no photophobia, no significant pain, and vision is normal. On examination, the conjunctiva is diffusely injected, with a mucopurulent discharge. The cornea is clear, and pupils are normal.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age (young adult)** – most common in children and young adults; viral (adenovirus) or bacterial (staph/strep) peak.
- **Acute onset (2 days)** – typical for infectious conjunctivitis; allergic can be recurrent or seasonal.
- **“Gritty” sensation, not pain** – conjunctival inflammation feels like sand; true pain suggests corneal involvement (keratitis) or deeper inflammation (uveitis/glaucoma).
- **“Eyelids stuck together in morning”** – mucopurulent discharge dries overnight; classic for bacterial conjunctivitis.
- **No photophobia** – key negative; photophobia is red flag for keratitis or iritis.
- **Normal vision** – if vision is reduced, think corneal oedema (glaucoma), keratitis, or uveitis.
- **Diffuse conjunctival injection** – pattern: more prominent peripherally, fades toward limbus (unlike ciliary flush – deeper red at limbus).
- **Clear cornea** – rules out corneal ulcer / keratitis.
- **Normal pupils** – rules out iritis (miotic, sluggish) and acute angle-closure glaucoma (mid-dilated, fixed).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Neonatal conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum)** – occurs in first month of life, can be chlamydial or gonococcal. Gonococcal: profuse purulent discharge, rapid onset (days 2-5), sight-threatening. Chlamydial: more subacute, watery/mucoid, weeks later. **Always think sexually transmitted infection.**
2. **Chronic conjunctivitis (>2 weeks)** – consider chlamydia (adults), molluscum contagiosum (viral inclusion bodies), or dry eye (aqueous deficiency / meibomian gland dysfunction).
3. **Contact lens-related red eye** – often sterile infiltrates (contact lens–induced acute red eye – CLARE) but can be bacterial keratitis. **Pain, photophobia, corneal opacity = emergency.** Do not dismiss as simple conjunctivitis.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva – the thin, translucent mucous membrane lining the inner eyelids (palpebral) and covering the anterior sclera (bulbar). The conjunctiva is highly vascular and immunologically active.

**Why it matters:**
- **Extremely common:** accounts for ~1% of primary care visits and is the most common cause of red eye in children and young adults.
- **Mostly benign and self-limiting:** but shares features with sight-threatening conditions – keratitis, acute glaucoma, anterior uveitis. Missing these can lead to permanent vision loss.
- **Common pitfalls:** overtreating viral conjunctivitis with antibiotics (no benefit, contributes to resistance), missing chlamydial infection (risk of corneal scarring, and partner treatment needed), failing to recognise an ocular emergency.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of conjunctival inflammation:**
The conjunctiva is a barrier and immune organ. When pathogens or allergens breach its surface, a cascade of vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and white cell recruitment occurs.

- **Viral infection (most common, adenovirus):** Adenovirus infects conjunctival epithelial cells → cell lysis → release of inflammatory cytokines → vasodilation (redness) and exudation (watery discharge). Lymphocytes infiltrate → **follicles** (lymphoid hyperplasia) seen on everted lid. The cornea may develop punctate epithelial keratitis (photophobia if involved).
- **Bacterial infection:** Bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) release toxins → neutrophil chemotaxis → purulent discharge (copious, green-yellow). Enzymes (proteases) cause epithelial sloughing – lids stick together. Toxins also dilate vessels → injection.
- **Allergic:** Mast cell degranulation (IgE-mediated) → histamine release → itching, watery discharge, **papillae** (tissue oedema with central vascular tuft) on tarsal conjunctiva. Venous congestion leads to chemosis (gelatinous swelling of bulbar conjunctiva).

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
- **Diffuse injection** → dilation of superficial conjunctival vessels due to inflammatory mediators.
- **Discharge type** → reflects the dominant cell type: viruses → lymphocytes → watery; bacteria → neutrophils → purulent; allergy → mast cells → stringy, mucoid.
- **Grittiness / foreign body sensation** → inflamed conjunctival surface rubs against lid.
- **Follicles** (clear, pale bumps on palpebral conjunctiva) → lymphoid hyperplasia (viral, chlamydial).
- **Papillae** (red, velvety, cobblestone pattern) → oedema with central vascular core (allergic, giant papillary conjunctivitis from contact lenses).
- **Preauricular lymph node** (tender) → viral (especially adenovirus) or chlamydial infection; bacterial rarely causes lymphadenopathy.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
The classic vignette – young adult, acute onset, mucopurulent discharge, morning stickiness – points to bacterial aetiology (neutrophils). Clear cornea and no photophobia mean no keratitis. No preauricular node suggests not adenovirus.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious – Viral** | Adenovirus (most common viral) | Watery discharge, preauricular node, recent upper respiratory infection |
| | Herpes simplex (HSV) | Unilateral, watery, dendritic ulcer on cornea (keratoconjunctivitis) – **emergency** |
| | Enterovirus / Coxsackie | Epidemic haemorrhagic conjunctivitis – subconjunctival haemorrhages |
| **Infectious – Bacterial** | *Staphylococcus aureus* (most common bacterial) | Purulent discharge, bilateral, sometimes blepharitis |
| | *Streptococcus pneumoniae* | Profuse discharge, children, often after URI |
| | *Haemophilus influenzae* | Children <5, often bilateral, with otitis media |
| | *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* (emergency) | Hyperacute, purulent, rapid onset (<24h), sexually active young adult |
| | *Chlamydia trachomatis* | Chronic, mucoid discharge, inclusion bodies, sexually active / neonate |
| **Allergic** | Seasonal allergic rhinitis | Itching, bilateral, watery, seasonal, associated sneezing. **Chemosis** |
| | Atopic / vernal keratoconjunctivitis | Children, severe itching, giant papillae, corneal shield ulcers – **refer** |
| **Irritant / Toxic** | Contact lens solution, chemicals, topical medications | History of exposure, diffuse injection, ± punctate keratitis |
| **Autoimmune / Systemic** | Reactive arthritis (Reiter’s) | Conjunctivitis + urethritis + arthritis |
| | Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) | Acute onset, mucous membrane involvement, skin blisters – **life-threatening** |
| | Ocular mucous membrane pemphigoid | Chronic, progressive symblepharon (lid-sclera adhesion) – rare but blinding |

**Most common cause overall:** Infectious conjunctivitis – viral (adenovirus) in adults, bacterial in children (especially *H. influenzae* and *S. pneumoniae*).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** There is no formal scoring system; diagnosis is clinical. The key is to distinguish simple conjunctivitis from corneal / intraocular pathology.

**Tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Slit lamp examination (essential)** | Conjunctival injection pattern, discharge, follicles/papillae, corneal clarity, anterior chamber cells/flare, pupil reaction | Differentiate conjunctivitis from keratitis, iritis, glaucoma | Diffuse injection + clear cornea + no AC cells = conjunctivitis. Focal injection + corneal opacity = keratitis. AC cells/flare = iritis. |
| **Fluorescein staining** | Corneal epithelial defects (dendrites in HSV, punctate erosions) | Suspect keratitis (pain, photophobia, decreased vision) | No uptake = cornea intact. Dendritic ulcer = HSV – **emergency, refer** |
| **Conjunctival swab for culture & sensitivity** | Bacterial pathogen (gram stain, culture) | Hyperacute purulent discharge, suspected gonorrhoea/chlamydia, neonatal, immunocompromised, treatment failure | Gram-negative diplococci = *Neisseria*. Chlamydial PCR or immunofluorescence. Routine uncomplicated – not required |
| **PCR for adenovirus / chlamydia** | Viral or chlamydial DNA | Suspected adenovirus (rapid, but rarely needed), chronic conjunctivitis | Confirms aetiology; may guide infection control (adenovirus highly contagious) |
| **Blood tests (rare)** | ESR, CRP, autoantibodies | Suspected autoimmune or reactive arthritis | Elevated ESR/CRP; specific autoantibodies based on suspicion |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **Clear cornea + normal pupil + no photophobia** = safe to diagnose conjunctivitis.
- **Fluorescein-positive dendritic ulcer** = HSV keratitis – rule out conjunctivitis, refer urgently.
- **Preauricular lymph node** = strongly suggests viral (adenovirus) or chlamydial aetiology.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (within first hour):**
- **Alleviate discomfort:** Artificial tears (hypromellose 0.3% or carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) every 1-2 hours as needed. Soothing, washes out irritants.
- **Hygiene education:** Wash hands after touching eyes, separate towels/pillowcases, avoid sharing makeup or contact lenses. In viral cases, patient is contagious for 10-14 days.
- **Remove contact lenses** if worn → switch to glasses. Can reintroduce after complete resolution.
- **No topical steroids** unless senior ophthalmologist prescribes (e.g., severe allergic / atopic keratoconjunctivitis). **Steroids can worsen HSV keratitis** → devastating.

**Ward / clinic (24-72h):**
- **Bacterial conjunctivitis – when to treat:** Purulent discharge, positive swab, or high-risk (immunocompromised, neonate). Options:
  - *Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops* 1 drop every 2 hours initially, then 4 times daily; OR *Fusidic acid 1% gel* twice daily. Both are bacteriostatic against gram-positives and some gram-negatives. Chloramphenicol cheaper, but theoretical risk of aplastic anaemia (extremely rare). Fusidic acid better for staphylococci.
  - Duration: 5-7 days. **Do not use routine antibiotics for all red eyes** – 80% of acute conjunctivitis is viral, and antibiotics don’t help.
- **Viral conjunctivitis – no specific treatment.** Supportive: cool compresses, artificial tears, strict hygiene. Advise GP follow-up if worsening.
- **Allergic conjunctivitis:** Oral antihistamine (e.g., *cetirizine 10 mg once daily*) plus topical antihistamine/ mast cell stabiliser (e.g., *sodium cromoglicate 2%* 1 drop 4 times daily). If severe, consider *olopatadine 0.1%* twice daily.
- **Chlamydial conjunctivitis:** *Oral azithromycin 1 g stat* (adult) or *doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days* (avoid in pregnancy/children). Treat partner(s). For neonates: oral erythromycin syrup.
- **Gonococcal conjunctivitis:** **Ophthalmological emergency.** *Ceftriaxone 1 g IM/IV stat* (adult) or 25-50 mg/kg in neonates. Frequent saline irrigation. Admit. **If not treated, can cause corneal perforation in hours.**

**Long-term considerations:**
- **Recurrent viral conjunctivitis:** Consider referral if keratitis develops.
- **Chronic conjunctivitis (>2 weeks):** Rule out chlamydia, molluscum, dry eye, or blepharitis. Lid hygiene for blepharitis: warm compresses + lid massage + *bacitracin/polymyxin* ointment at bedtime.
- **Giant papillary conjunctivitis:** Discontinue contact lens, consider *lodoxamide* or *cyclosporine* drops.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe topical antibiotics for watery/clear discharge – likely viral.
- Do not use combination steroid-antibiotic drops (e.g., Maxitrol) unless instructed by ophthalmologist.
- Do not patch an infected eye – promotes microbial growth.
- Do not forget to check for contact lens use – missing keratitis is dangerous.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Conjunctivitis | Keratitis (corneal infection/inflammation) | Anterior Uveitis (iritis) | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Gritty, superficial | Moderate to severe, sharp, may radiate | Deep ache, photophobia | Severe, acute, headache, nausea |
| **Redness** | Diffuse, more peripherally | Focal, often at site of ulcer | **Circumcorneal (ciliary flush)** – deep red ring | Diffuse, with corneal oedema (hazy) |
| **Vision** | Normal (or slight blur from discharge) | Decreased if central cornea involved | Decreased, blurry | Acute decrease (hand motion / counting fingers) |
| **Pupil** | Normal | Normal or slightly reactive | Small, irregular, sluggish, may have posterior synechiae | Mid-dilated, fixed, oval |
| **Discharge** | Watery (viral) / purulent (bacterial) | Watery or purulent if infected | None | None (tearing only) |
| **Photophobia** | No | Yes (especially if epithelial defect) | Yes (mild to severe) | Yes |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Normal | Normal | Usually normal (can be low or high) | **Very high** (40-80 mmHg) |
| **Key differentiating sign** | Clear cornea, normal vision | Corneal opacity on slit lamp (+ fluorescein uptake) | Cells + flare in anterior chamber, no corneal stain | Shallow anterior chamber, corneal oedema, fixed mid-dilated pupil |

**Mnemonic for red eye causes: “CSAG”** – Conjunctivitis, Scratch (corneal abrasion/keratitis), Acute glaucoma, anterior uveitis (Iritis). The last two are eye emergencies.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Corneal perforation (in gonococcal or severe bacterial)** | Pain worsening, loss of vision, eye feels soft | Seidel test positive (fluorescein streaming) | **Emergency** – eye shield, no pressure. Urgent ophthalmology. IV antibiotics + surgical repair |
| **HSV keratitis sequelae** | Recurrent dendritic ulcers → corneal scarring, neovascularisation, neurotrophic ulcers | Stromal haze, reduced sensation (trigeminal nerve) | Long-term antivirals (acyclovir 400 mg po BD). Refer to cornea specialist |
| **Corneal scarring (chlamydial trachoma)** | Chronic, conjunctival scarring, trichiasis (in-turned lashes), entropion | Scarring on everted lids, corneal opacity | Lid surgery, antibiotics (azithromycin mass drug administration in endemic areas). Blindness preventable |
| **Symblepharon (in SJS or mucous membrane pemphigoid)** | Adhesions between lid and globe, restricted eye movement | Band-like attachments on slit lamp | Refer to corneal / oculoplastics. Topical steroids, immunosuppression |
| **Secondary bacterial infection of viral conjunctivitis** | Worsening discharge after initial watery phase | Change in discharge character, increased redness | Culture, treat as bacterial |
| **Acute corneal oedema (if misdiagnosed glaucoma)** | Pain, nausea, redness, cloudy cornea | IOP >40, fixed pupil | Urgent IOP-lowering – see management of acute glaucoma (pilocarpine, acetazolamide, IV mannitol) |

**Late-presentation patterns (what examiners test):**
- “A 65-year-old woman with red, painful eye for 3 days, vomiting, cloudy cornea, pupil fixed.” → Acute angle-closure glaucoma (not conjunctivitis). **Do not instil pilocarpine if IOP >60 and corneal oedema** – iris is ischaemic, pilocarpine won’t work. Immediate IV acetazolamide and topical beta-blocker.
- “A neonate with profuse purulent discharge day 3 of life.” → Gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. **Do not delay culture; start IV ceftriaxone.**
- “A contact lens wearer with red, painful eye, photophobia, small white corneal infiltrate.” → Bacterial keratitis (often *Pseudomonas*). **Do not use steroid drops. Gram stain + culture. Start topical fluoroquinolone (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3% every hour).**

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
1. **“RED EYE” – Quick differential:**
   - **R** – rapid onset: conjunctivitis vs. acute glaucoma
   - **E** – eye pain: is it superficial (conjunctivitis) or deep (iritis, glaucoma)?
   - **D** – discharge: mucopurulent = bacterial; watery = viral or allergy
   - **E** – examine: fluorescein, slit lamp, pupil, IOP
2. **“VIB” – For conjunctivitis discharge pattern:**
   - **V**iral – Watery, clear, stringy
   - **I**rritant – Watery, bilateral
   - **B**acterial – Purulent, thick, green/yellow
3. **“CLEAR” – No-go signs for primary care (refer):**
   - **C** – Corneal opacity
   - **L** – Light (photophobia)
   - **E** – Eye pain (moderate/severe)
   - **A** – Anterior chamber shallow or cells
   - **R** – Reduced vision

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **The “gritty” sensation is conjunctivitis; the “scratchy sandpaper” is keratitis; the “deep ache” is iritis.**
2. **Preauricular node = viral (adenovirus) until proven otherwise.**
3. **Bilateral conjunctivitis in a newborn – can be chlamydial (weeks old) or gonococcal (days). Always culture.**
4. **Contact lens wearers with red eye: treat as bacterial keratitis until proven negative. Do not patch.**
5. **Eye pain with nausea/vomiting = acute glaucoma until proven otherwise.**
6. **Topical steroids in a red eye are dangerous – they can worsen HSV keratitis, fungal keratitis, and mask a corneal perforation.**
7. **The majority of acute conjunctivitis in immunocompetent adults is viral; antibiotics are unnecessary and cause resistance.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Missing photophobia as a red flag – “patient says no light sensitivity” in question stem may be the clue to conjunctivitis vs. iritis.
- Prescribing chloramphenicol for a neonate with conjunctivitis without ruling out gonorrhoea/chlamydia – culture mandatory.
- Thinking bilateral red eye = allergic when it could be viral (adenovirus often bilateral, especially in children).
- Forgetting to check for herpetic skin lesions – vesicular rash on eyelid or forehead indicates shingles (HZO) → urgent antivirals.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ophthalmology (same day):**
- **Any vision loss** – Snellen <6/12 or subjective decrease.
- **Severe pain** – especially with nausea/vomiting (suggests acute glaucoma).
- **Corneal opacity** on examination – fluorescein uptake or hazy cornea.
- **Suspected HSV keratitis** – dendritic ulcer.
- **Hyperacute purulent discharge** – gonococcal until proven otherwise.
- **Traumatic red eye** – recent injury.
- **Contact lens wearer** – always refer if pain or photophobia.
- **Neonatal conjunctivitis** – refer and culture urgently.
- **Systemic signs** – fever, rash (SJS), joint pain (reactive arthritis).

**Specific thresholds (escalation to ICU / crash team very rare):**
- **NEWS2 >5** – suggests overwhelming infection (e.g., gonococcal sepsis).
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – with signs of systemic infection.
- **GCS <15** – if associated with meningism (meningococcal conjunctivitis can herald meningitis).
- **Anaphylaxis** – rare with topical treatments, but if after antibiotic drop → call crash team.

**Red flags for immediate ophthalmology referral (call registrar):**
- Uveitis (anterior chamber cells)
- Acute glaucoma (IOP >21, shallow AC, fixed mid-dilated pupil)
- Corneal ulcer / perforation (Seidel positive)
- Suspected scleritis (deep boring pain, bluish hue, tender globe – much less common but more serious)

**When to call senior in hospital:**
- If you are a junior and cannot perform a slit lamp exam OR are unsure of the diagnosis.
- If patient requires hospital admission (neonatal gonococcal, SJS, bilateral severe uveitis).
- If you have to administer topical steroids but are not trained to monitor complications.

**Bottom line:** Conjunctivitis is common and usually easy to manage, but the one-shot teaching point is **never dismiss a red eye as “just conjunctivitis” until you have ruled out corneal involvement, elevated IOP, and intraocular inflammation.** The slit lamp is your best friend; if you don’t have one, refer.

rawMarkdown:
# Conjunctivitis

**One-liner:** Acute or chronic inflammation of the conjunctiva, most often infectious or allergic; common, usually self-limiting, but mimics sight-threatening emergencies.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 22-year-old medical student presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of red, gritty left eye. She reports waking with eyelids stuck together this morning. There is no photophobia, no significant pain, and vision is normal. On examination, the conjunctiva is diffusely injected, with a mucopurulent discharge. The cornea is clear, and pupils are normal.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age (young adult)** – most common in children and young adults; viral (adenovirus) or bacterial (staph/strep) peak.
- **Acute onset (2 days)** – typical for infectious conjunctivitis; allergic can be recurrent or seasonal.
- **“Gritty” sensation, not pain** – conjunctival inflammation feels like sand; true pain suggests corneal involvement (keratitis) or deeper inflammation (uveitis/glaucoma).
- **“Eyelids stuck together in morning”** – mucopurulent discharge dries overnight; classic for bacterial conjunctivitis.
- **No photophobia** – key negative; photophobia is red flag for keratitis or iritis.
- **Normal vision** – if vision is reduced, think corneal oedema (glaucoma), keratitis, or uveitis.
- **Diffuse conjunctival injection** – pattern: more prominent peripherally, fades toward limbus (unlike ciliary flush – deeper red at limbus).
- **Clear cornea** – rules out corneal ulcer / keratitis.
- **Normal pupils** – rules out iritis (miotic, sluggish) and acute angle-closure glaucoma (mid-dilated, fixed).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Neonatal conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum)** – occurs in first month of life, can be chlamydial or gonococcal. Gonococcal: profuse purulent discharge, rapid onset (days 2-5), sight-threatening. Chlamydial: more subacute, watery/mucoid, weeks later. **Always think sexually transmitted infection.**
2. **Chronic conjunctivitis (>2 weeks)** – consider chlamydia (adults), molluscum contagiosum (viral inclusion bodies), or dry eye (aqueous deficiency / meibomian gland dysfunction).
3. **Contact lens-related red eye** – often sterile infiltrates (contact lens–induced acute red eye – CLARE) but can be bacterial keratitis. **Pain, photophobia, corneal opacity = emergency.** Do not dismiss as simple conjunctivitis.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva – the thin, translucent mucous membrane lining the inner eyelids (palpebral) and covering the anterior sclera (bulbar). The conjunctiva is highly vascular and immunologically active.

**Why it matters:**
- **Extremely common:** accounts for ~1% of primary care visits and is the most common cause of red eye in children and young adults.
- **Mostly benign and self-limiting:** but shares features with sight-threatening conditions – keratitis, acute glaucoma, anterior uveitis. Missing these can lead to permanent vision loss.
- **Common pitfalls:** overtreating viral conjunctivitis with antibiotics (no benefit, contributes to resistance), missing chlamydial infection (risk of corneal scarring, and partner treatment needed), failing to recognise an ocular emergency.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of conjunctival inflammation:**
The conjunctiva is a barrier and immune organ. When pathogens or allergens breach its surface, a cascade of vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and white cell recruitment occurs.

- **Viral infection (most common, adenovirus):** Adenovirus infects conjunctival epithelial cells → cell lysis → release of inflammatory cytokines → vasodilation (redness) and exudation (watery discharge). Lymphocytes infiltrate → **follicles** (lymphoid hyperplasia) seen on everted lid. The cornea may develop punctate epithelial keratitis (photophobia if involved).
- **Bacterial infection:** Bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) release toxins → neutrophil chemotaxis → purulent discharge (copious, green-yellow). Enzymes (proteases) cause epithelial sloughing – lids stick together. Toxins also dilate vessels → injection.
- **Allergic:** Mast cell degranulation (IgE-mediated) → histamine release → itching, watery discharge, **papillae** (tissue oedema with central vascular tuft) on tarsal conjunctiva. Venous congestion leads to chemosis (gelatinous swelling of bulbar conjunctiva).

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
- **Diffuse injection** → dilation of superficial conjunctival vessels due to inflammatory mediators.
- **Discharge type** → reflects the dominant cell type: viruses → lymphocytes → watery; bacteria → neutrophils → purulent; allergy → mast cells → stringy, mucoid.
- **Grittiness / foreign body sensation** → inflamed conjunctival surface rubs against lid.
- **Follicles** (clear, pale bumps on palpebral conjunctiva) → lymphoid hyperplasia (viral, chlamydial).
- **Papillae** (red, velvety, cobblestone pattern) → oedema with central vascular core (allergic, giant papillary conjunctivitis from contact lenses).
- **Preauricular lymph node** (tender) → viral (especially adenovirus) or chlamydial infection; bacterial rarely causes lymphadenopathy.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
The classic vignette – young adult, acute onset, mucopurulent discharge, morning stickiness – points to bacterial aetiology (neutrophils). Clear cornea and no photophobia mean no keratitis. No preauricular node suggests not adenovirus.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious – Viral** | Adenovirus (most common viral) | Watery discharge, preauricular node, recent upper respiratory infection |
| | Herpes simplex (HSV) | Unilateral, watery, dendritic ulcer on cornea (keratoconjunctivitis) – **emergency** |
| | Enterovirus / Coxsackie | Epidemic haemorrhagic conjunctivitis – subconjunctival haemorrhages |
| **Infectious – Bacterial** | *Staphylococcus aureus* (most common bacterial) | Purulent discharge, bilateral, sometimes blepharitis |
| | *Streptococcus pneumoniae* | Profuse discharge, children, often after URI |
| | *Haemophilus influenzae* | Children <5, often bilateral, with otitis media |
| | *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* (emergency) | Hyperacute, purulent, rapid onset (<24h), sexually active young adult |
| | *Chlamydia trachomatis* | Chronic, mucoid discharge, inclusion bodies, sexually active / neonate |
| **Allergic** | Seasonal allergic rhinitis | Itching, bilateral, watery, seasonal, associated sneezing. **Chemosis** |
| | Atopic / vernal keratoconjunctivitis | Children, severe itching, giant papillae, corneal shield ulcers – **refer** |
| **Irritant / Toxic** | Contact lens solution, chemicals, topical medications | History of exposure, diffuse injection, ± punctate keratitis |
| **Autoimmune / Systemic** | Reactive arthritis (Reiter’s) | Conjunctivitis + urethritis + arthritis |
| | Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) | Acute onset, mucous membrane involvement, skin blisters – **life-threatening** |
| | Ocular mucous membrane pemphigoid | Chronic, progressive symblepharon (lid-sclera adhesion) – rare but blinding |

**Most common cause overall:** Infectious conjunctivitis – viral (adenovirus) in adults, bacterial in children (especially *H. influenzae* and *S. pneumoniae*).

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** There is no formal scoring system; diagnosis is clinical. The key is to distinguish simple conjunctivitis from corneal / intraocular pathology.

**Tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Slit lamp examination (essential)** | Conjunctival injection pattern, discharge, follicles/papillae, corneal clarity, anterior chamber cells/flare, pupil reaction | Differentiate conjunctivitis from keratitis, iritis, glaucoma | Diffuse injection + clear cornea + no AC cells = conjunctivitis. Focal injection + corneal opacity = keratitis. AC cells/flare = iritis. |
| **Fluorescein staining** | Corneal epithelial defects (dendrites in HSV, punctate erosions) | Suspect keratitis (pain, photophobia, decreased vision) | No uptake = cornea intact. Dendritic ulcer = HSV – **emergency, refer** |
| **Conjunctival swab for culture & sensitivity** | Bacterial pathogen (gram stain, culture) | Hyperacute purulent discharge, suspected gonorrhoea/chlamydia, neonatal, immunocompromised, treatment failure | Gram-negative diplococci = *Neisseria*. Chlamydial PCR or immunofluorescence. Routine uncomplicated – not required |
| **PCR for adenovirus / chlamydia** | Viral or chlamydial DNA | Suspected adenovirus (rapid, but rarely needed), chronic conjunctivitis | Confirms aetiology; may guide infection control (adenovirus highly contagious) |
| **Blood tests (rare)** | ESR, CRP, autoantibodies | Suspected autoimmune or reactive arthritis | Elevated ESR/CRP; specific autoantibodies based on suspicion |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **Clear cornea + normal pupil + no photophobia** = safe to diagnose conjunctivitis.
- **Fluorescein-positive dendritic ulcer** = HSV keratitis – rule out conjunctivitis, refer urgently.
- **Preauricular lymph node** = strongly suggests viral (adenovirus) or chlamydial aetiology.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (within first hour):**
- **Alleviate discomfort:** Artificial tears (hypromellose 0.3% or carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) every 1-2 hours as needed. Soothing, washes out irritants.
- **Hygiene education:** Wash hands after touching eyes, separate towels/pillowcases, avoid sharing makeup or contact lenses. In viral cases, patient is contagious for 10-14 days.
- **Remove contact lenses** if worn → switch to glasses. Can reintroduce after complete resolution.
- **No topical steroids** unless senior ophthalmologist prescribes (e.g., severe allergic / atopic keratoconjunctivitis). **Steroids can worsen HSV keratitis** → devastating.

**Ward / clinic (24-72h):**
- **Bacterial conjunctivitis – when to treat:** Purulent discharge, positive swab, or high-risk (immunocompromised, neonate). Options:
  - *Chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops* 1 drop every 2 hours initially, then 4 times daily; OR *Fusidic acid 1% gel* twice daily. Both are bacteriostatic against gram-positives and some gram-negatives. Chloramphenicol cheaper, but theoretical risk of aplastic anaemia (extremely rare). Fusidic acid better for staphylococci.
  - Duration: 5-7 days. **Do not use routine antibiotics for all red eyes** – 80% of acute conjunctivitis is viral, and antibiotics don’t help.
- **Viral conjunctivitis – no specific treatment.** Supportive: cool compresses, artificial tears, strict hygiene. Advise GP follow-up if worsening.
- **Allergic conjunctivitis:** Oral antihistamine (e.g., *cetirizine 10 mg once daily*) plus topical antihistamine/ mast cell stabiliser (e.g., *sodium cromoglicate 2%* 1 drop 4 times daily). If severe, consider *olopatadine 0.1%* twice daily.
- **Chlamydial conjunctivitis:** *Oral azithromycin 1 g stat* (adult) or *doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days* (avoid in pregnancy/children). Treat partner(s). For neonates: oral erythromycin syrup.
- **Gonococcal conjunctivitis:** **Ophthalmological emergency.** *Ceftriaxone 1 g IM/IV stat* (adult) or 25-50 mg/kg in neonates. Frequent saline irrigation. Admit. **If not treated, can cause corneal perforation in hours.**

**Long-term considerations:**
- **Recurrent viral conjunctivitis:** Consider referral if keratitis develops.
- **Chronic conjunctivitis (>2 weeks):** Rule out chlamydia, molluscum, dry eye, or blepharitis. Lid hygiene for blepharitis: warm compresses + lid massage + *bacitracin/polymyxin* ointment at bedtime.
- **Giant papillary conjunctivitis:** Discontinue contact lens, consider *lodoxamide* or *cyclosporine* drops.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe topical antibiotics for watery/clear discharge – likely viral.
- Do not use combination steroid-antibiotic drops (e.g., Maxitrol) unless instructed by ophthalmologist.
- Do not patch an infected eye – promotes microbial growth.
- Do not forget to check for contact lens use – missing keratitis is dangerous.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Conjunctivitis | Keratitis (corneal infection/inflammation) | Anterior Uveitis (iritis) | Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Gritty, superficial | Moderate to severe, sharp, may radiate | Deep ache, photophobia | Severe, acute, headache, nausea |
| **Redness** | Diffuse, more peripherally | Focal, often at site of ulcer | **Circumcorneal (ciliary flush)** – deep red ring | Diffuse, with corneal oedema (hazy) |
| **Vision** | Normal (or slight blur from discharge) | Decreased if central cornea involved | Decreased, blurry | Acute decrease (hand motion / counting fingers) |
| **Pupil** | Normal | Normal or slightly reactive | Small, irregular, sluggish, may have posterior synechiae | Mid-dilated, fixed, oval |
| **Discharge** | Watery (viral) / purulent (bacterial) | Watery or purulent if infected | None | None (tearing only) |
| **Photophobia** | No | Yes (especially if epithelial defect) | Yes (mild to severe) | Yes |
| **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** | Normal | Normal | Usually normal (can be low or high) | **Very high** (40-80 mmHg) |
| **Key differentiating sign** | Clear cornea, normal vision | Corneal opacity on slit lamp (+ fluorescein uptake) | Cells + flare in anterior chamber, no corneal stain | Shallow anterior chamber, corneal oedema, fixed mid-dilated pupil |

**Mnemonic for red eye causes: “CSAG”** – Conjunctivitis, Scratch (corneal abrasion/keratitis), Acute glaucoma, anterior uveitis (Iritis). The last two are eye emergencies.

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Corneal perforation (in gonococcal or severe bacterial)** | Pain worsening, loss of vision, eye feels soft | Seidel test positive (fluorescein streaming) | **Emergency** – eye shield, no pressure. Urgent ophthalmology. IV antibiotics + surgical repair |
| **HSV keratitis sequelae** | Recurrent dendritic ulcers → corneal scarring, neovascularisation, neurotrophic ulcers | Stromal haze, reduced sensation (trigeminal nerve) | Long-term antivirals (acyclovir 400 mg po BD). Refer to cornea specialist |
| **Corneal scarring (chlamydial trachoma)** | Chronic, conjunctival scarring, trichiasis (in-turned lashes), entropion | Scarring on everted lids, corneal opacity | Lid surgery, antibiotics (azithromycin mass drug administration in endemic areas). Blindness preventable |
| **Symblepharon (in SJS or mucous membrane pemphigoid)** | Adhesions between lid and globe, restricted eye movement | Band-like attachments on slit lamp | Refer to corneal / oculoplastics. Topical steroids, immunosuppression |
| **Secondary bacterial infection of viral conjunctivitis** | Worsening discharge after initial watery phase | Change in discharge character, increased redness | Culture, treat as bacterial |
| **Acute corneal oedema (if misdiagnosed glaucoma)** | Pain, nausea, redness, cloudy cornea | IOP >40, fixed pupil | Urgent IOP-lowering – see management of acute glaucoma (pilocarpine, acetazolamide, IV mannitol) |

**Late-presentation patterns (what examiners test):**
- “A 65-year-old woman with red, painful eye for 3 days, vomiting, cloudy cornea, pupil fixed.” → Acute angle-closure glaucoma (not conjunctivitis). **Do not instil pilocarpine if IOP >60 and corneal oedema** – iris is ischaemic, pilocarpine won’t work. Immediate IV acetazolamide and topical beta-blocker.
- “A neonate with profuse purulent discharge day 3 of life.” → Gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. **Do not delay culture; start IV ceftriaxone.**
- “A contact lens wearer with red, painful eye, photophobia, small white corneal infiltrate.” → Bacterial keratitis (often *Pseudomonas*). **Do not use steroid drops. Gram stain + culture. Start topical fluoroquinolone (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3% every hour).**

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
1. **“RED EYE” – Quick differential:**
   - **R** – rapid onset: conjunctivitis vs. acute glaucoma
   - **E** – eye pain: is it superficial (conjunctivitis) or deep (iritis, glaucoma)?
   - **D** – discharge: mucopurulent = bacterial; watery = viral or allergy
   - **E** – examine: fluorescein, slit lamp, pupil, IOP
2. **“VIB” – For conjunctivitis discharge pattern:**
   - **V**iral – Watery, clear, stringy
   - **I**rritant – Watery, bilateral
   - **B**acterial – Purulent, thick, green/yellow
3. **“CLEAR” – No-go signs for primary care (refer):**
   - **C** – Corneal opacity
   - **L** – Light (photophobia)
   - **E** – Eye pain (moderate/severe)
   - **A** – Anterior chamber shallow or cells
   - **R** – Reduced vision

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **The “gritty” sensation is conjunctivitis; the “scratchy sandpaper” is keratitis; the “deep ache” is iritis.**
2. **Preauricular node = viral (adenovirus) until proven otherwise.**
3. **Bilateral conjunctivitis in a newborn – can be chlamydial (weeks old) or gonococcal (days). Always culture.**
4. **Contact lens wearers with red eye: treat as bacterial keratitis until proven negative. Do not patch.**
5. **Eye pain with nausea/vomiting = acute glaucoma until proven otherwise.**
6. **Topical steroids in a red eye are dangerous – they can worsen HSV keratitis, fungal keratitis, and mask a corneal perforation.**
7. **The majority of acute conjunctivitis in immunocompetent adults is viral; antibiotics are unnecessary and cause resistance.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Missing photophobia as a red flag – “patient says no light sensitivity” in question stem may be the clue to conjunctivitis vs. iritis.
- Prescribing chloramphenicol for a neonate with conjunctivitis without ruling out gonorrhoea/chlamydia – culture mandatory.
- Thinking bilateral red eye = allergic when it could be viral (adenovirus often bilateral, especially in children).
- Forgetting to check for herpetic skin lesions – vesicular rash on eyelid or forehead indicates shingles (HZO) → urgent antivirals.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ophthalmology (same day):**
- **Any vision loss** – Snellen <6/12 or subjective decrease.
- **Severe pain** – especially with nausea/vomiting (suggests acute glaucoma).
- **Corneal opacity** on examination – fluorescein uptake or hazy cornea.
- **Suspected HSV keratitis** – dendritic ulcer.
- **Hyperacute purulent discharge** – gonococcal until proven otherwise.
- **Traumatic red eye** – recent injury.
- **Contact lens wearer** – always refer if pain or photophobia.
- **Neonatal conjunctivitis** – refer and culture urgently.
- **Systemic signs** – fever, rash (SJS), joint pain (reactive arthritis).

**Specific thresholds (escalation to ICU / crash team very rare):**
- **NEWS2 >5** – suggests overwhelming infection (e.g., gonococcal sepsis).
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – with signs of systemic infection.
- **GCS <15** – if associated with meningism (meningococcal conjunctivitis can herald meningitis).
- **Anaphylaxis** – rare with topical treatments, but if after antibiotic drop → call crash team.

**Red flags for immediate ophthalmology referral (call registrar):**
- Uveitis (anterior chamber cells)
- Acute glaucoma (IOP >21, shallow AC, fixed mid-dilated pupil)
- Corneal ulcer / perforation (Seidel positive)
- Suspected scleritis (deep boring pain, bluish hue, tender globe – much less common but more serious)

**When to call senior in hospital:**
- If you are a junior and cannot perform a slit lamp exam OR are unsure of the diagnosis.
- If patient requires hospital admission (neonatal gonococcal, SJS, bilateral severe uveitis).
- If you have to administer topical steroids but are not trained to monitor complications.

**Bottom line:** Conjunctivitis is common and usually easy to manage, but the one-shot teaching point is **never dismiss a red eye as “just conjunctivitis” until you have ruled out corneal involvement, elevated IOP, and intraocular inflammation.** The slit lamp is your best friend; if you don’t have one, refer.
